ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for steamed cuttlefish as a prepared food product; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared seafood dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of steamed cuttlefish is approximately 150g, equivalent to a medium-sized whole cuttlefish or a generous portion of sliced cuttlefish served as a side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed Cuttlefish — Singapore Health Promotion Board Food Composition Database (HPB Foodo, Food ID F03050025)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for steamed cuttlefish, Food ID F03050025. This is an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods. All core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) and reported micronutrients (sodium, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, dietary fibre) are taken from this primary source with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (0.3g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cuttlefish (NDB 15149) as a secondary reference, as HPB does not report saturated fat fractions separately; this estimation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — steamed cuttlefish contains negligible carbohydrates (1.64g/100g); reference: University of Sydney GI Database methodology excludes foods with insufficient carbohydrate for GI testing
gi_source.source_match_notes: Steamed cuttlefish contains only 1.64g carbohydrate per 100g. The University of Sydney GI Database methodology requires a minimum of 50g available carbohydrate per test portion for valid GI measurement; this threshold cannot be met for steamed cuttlefish at any realistic serving size. Accordingly, glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 as the carbohydrate contribution is clinically negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 744mg/100g — significantly exceeds the 600mg/100g threshold for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Steamed cuttlefish contains 744mg sodium per 100g, well above the 600mg/100g upper limit for hypertension-safe foods; individuals with hypertension should limit portions to 50–80g, avoid adding soy sauce or any seasoning, and note that the high potassium content (637mg/100g) provides partial mitigation but does not override the sodium concern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (1.64g/100g) and very high protein (32.48g/100g) with no glycaemic spike risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed cuttlefish is highly suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its negligible carbohydrate content (1.64g/100g), zero sugar, and very high protein (32.48g/100g) which supports satiety and blood glucose stability; however, individuals with combined diabetes and dyslipidaemia should moderate portions to 100–150g given the elevated cholesterol content of 224mg/100g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol (224mg/100g) and elevated sodium (744mg/100g) offset by very low total fat (1.4g/100g) and low saturated fat (0.3g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed cuttlefish has very low total fat (1.4g/100g) and low saturated fat (0.3g/100g), which are favourable for cardiovascular health, but the high dietary cholesterol (224mg/100g) and elevated sodium (744mg/100g) require moderation; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portions to 80–100g, avoid added salt or sauces, and consume as part of a broader heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Exceptionally high iron (10.84mg/100g) and calcium (180mg/100g) beneficial for RA; sodium (744mg/100g) may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Steamed cuttlefish provides exceptional iron (10.84mg/100g) and calcium (180mg/100g) which support RA patients on corticosteroids, and the steamed preparation avoids AGE formation; however, sodium at 744mg/100g may exacerbate RA-related tissue swelling and portions should be strictly limited to 50–80g, with high cholesterol (224mg/100g) warranting moderation in patients on lipid-affecting medications.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed cuttlefish is a minimally processed seafood dish prepared by steaming whole or sliced cuttlefish (Sepia species) without added oil or seasoning, resulting in translucent white flesh with a firm, smooth, and slightly chewy texture. It is a staple protein source in Singaporean and Chinese cuisine, commonly served as a side dish at hawker centres, zi char stalls, and home meals. Nutritionally, steamed cuttlefish is exceptionally high in protein (32.48g/100g) and very low in fat (1.4g/100g) and carbohydrates (1.64g/100g), making it one of the leanest seafood options available. It is notably rich in iron (10.84mg/100g), calcium (180mg/100g), potassium (637mg/100g), and cholesterol (224mg/100g). The sodium content is elevated at 744mg/100g, which is a significant consideration for individuals managing hypertension or rheumatoid arthritis. Steamed cuttlefish is an excellent choice for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its negligible carbohydrate content and high protein, but its cholesterol and sodium levels require moderation for cardiovascular and hypertension patients. The steamed preparation method avoids the formation of advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) associated with high-heat cooking, making it preferable to fried or grilled alternatives for inflammatory conditions.
common_user_queries: How much protein is in steamed cuttlefish?
Is steamed cuttlefish high in sodium?
Is cuttlefish good for high blood pressure?
清蒸墨鱼 营养成分

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F03050025) with a confidence of 0.95. The food is an exact match in the primary regional source for Singapore foods. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a documented secondary reference.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for steamed cuttlefish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F03050025 as of 2026-04-16; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15149) was consulted as a secondary reference but does not provide all these fractions for the steamed preparation specifically. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — steamed cuttlefish contains no added or intrinsic sugars. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — cuttlefish is an animal product containing no dietary fibre. saturated_fat_g of 0.3g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cuttlefish entry (NDB 15149) as a secondary reference, as HPB does not report saturated fat fractions separately. Glycemic index is not applicable: steamed cuttlefish contains only 1.64g carbohydrate per 100g; GI testing requires a minimum of 50g available carbohydrate per test portion per University of Sydney GI Database methodology, which cannot be achieved at any realistic serving size; glycemic_index is set to null per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Sodium at 744mg/100g likely reflects natural cuttlefish sodium content plus any minimal processing; no added salt is assumed in the steamed preparation. Cuttlefish is classified as a mollusc and may trigger shellfish/mollusc allergies.
